rationale: The homes at this Spring, TX property display classic Texas suburban hip-roof construction. Shadow lengths cast from eave edges onto the ground are moderate — neither the short stubs of a 4:12 nor the elongated projections of an 8:12+ roof — pointing to a mid-range slope. The ratio of visible roof surface (nadir eave-to-ridge distance) to overall building footprint is consistent with a 6:12 pitch: the ridge lines are clearly defined but occupy a meaningful fraction of the plan width, ruling out very shallow (4:12) or steep (8:12+) slopes. Valley and hip intersection shadows show well-defined but not dramatic depth, further corroborating a 6:12 estimate. The southern Texas climate and architectural style of the neighborhood (multi-plane hip roofs on large suburban homes) are also strongly associated with 6:12 as the dominant pitch in this region.
